agent1_raw: The comparison is invalid because it uses different transaction feeds (settlement batch vs authorization stream), different label maturity rules (full stream vs 60-day settled), a Q4 threshold tuned on Q4 data, and bundles the model with a new pipeline. All four of these must be harmonized before a replacement claim can be made.
agent2_raw: Four flaws: (1) settlement feed vs authorization stream baseline; (2) full dispute stream vs 60-day settled label maturation; (3) threshold selected and evaluated on the same Q4 data; (4) model and pipeline changed simultaneously. The packet cannot attribute the 3.2-point precision gain to the graph model alone.
agent3_raw: The challenger is evaluated on a different transaction population with a different label definition, at a threshold selected on the reporting quarter, while simultaneously changing the model architecture and the pipeline. None of these is innocuous; together they make the comparison uninterpretable as a model quality test.
synthesis_raw: Three agents converge on critique_wins. The four defects — feed mismatch, label maturity mismatch, Q4 threshold self-selection, and model-plus-pipeline bundling — are consistently identified. No agent incorrectly challenges PR-AUC for fraud or merchant alias consolidation. No empirical test resolves these design-level problems; critique_wins because the current comparison cannot fairly establish model superiority.
all_issues_raised: Transaction feed changed from settlement batch to deduplicated authorization stream
Label maturation changed from full dispute stream to 60-day settled-only
Q4 threshold calibrated and reported on Q4 data — circular
Model and pipeline changed together — gains not separably attributable
